speaker: interviewer
text: Ben Roter. Before we continue with the story, I'd like to ask you two questions. One is about Ere Schupper. Did he know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the other question is-- I haven't heard his name. Do you know what happened with [? Angia ?] [? Bidelski? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I know what happened with [? Bidelski. ?] When I was working in Klevan for the Schupper, see, some Polacks, they came to work in Klevan and they told me they killed-- they find [? Angia ?] [? Bidelski ?] in the woods, and they put him a grenade, and they killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they used to come-- the Polacks, they used to come from Novazokuw, Stadazokuw. They used to come to Klevan to work. And they know that-- they know me because I used to go out in the nighttime to bread, you know? And they told me that [? Angia ?] [? Bidelski ?] is killed, and they find him in the woods there and they kill him. That's the reason I know that [? Angia ?] [? Bidelski ?] is dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, if you could continue with your story. You're in the castle, imprisoned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When they hit me-- I fell down on the floor, and the German guy told the police to give me a little bit water to wake up. I woke up.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, why did you lie? Why did you lie? You said you're not Jewish. You are Jewish. They took off my pants and they saw right away that I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put me in-- they went with me in the castle and they put me in the jail. They put me in the jail in the castle. They made from that castle, they made a jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went and I fell down. I fell down. I said what can I do? This going to be my end. I was three years-- I was thinking that I'm three years already survived. The last three more months the Russian are close to me and I'm going to die. There's nothing. I was very surprised.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was laying, and laying, and laying. They took off my shoes. I was without shoes, without boots, without a jacket, without nothing. Only they left of me my little sweater, my little sweater. I picked up my head and I saw all of the bars was missing. One of the bars was missing I say well, my God. You know, that's my luck. I say I will try to take out my head. If my head goes out, my whole body will go out. I know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went out. And over there was a big horse, a Belgium horse in that where I was laying on the floor. I went-- first off I went, I took a few old pieces bread from the horses. I ate it. My belly was full. So I went up and I tried to take it out, my head through the bar, what was missing. Luckily. Luckily, I see my head goes out. My head goes out, so I don't worry for my body. OK. My body. So I took my sweater-- if you want-- can I take my sweater? We'll do it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I took my little sweater. I tore off from the half, half and half, and I put it on my shoe-- on my feet because I was naked. I was just like that. And I went up. And I took out my head. And then I went out with my body, and I was outside from the jail, outside from the castle. And I went down. I jumped down. It was very, very cold. When I stepped on the snow, it was-- hearing. Somebody could hear, because that was-- the snow was so cold. I went to the door, where they took me in. I went about 20 feet, and I went-- and I heard that somebody tried to open the door, the gate, the door. Right away on the road, right away I wanted to run. So I went on the run. They didn't know that was a dog or that was a person. They didn't know who ran away. So I went on the field, the field that was about three kilometer from the woods where I want to go, or four kilometer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you one question. You said you were naked. But did you mean that you were shoeless, or were you naked?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not naked. Just without shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Without shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And without a jacket. So I ran with [INAUDIBLE] and I went on the field. The field was so high with snow, because at that time was-- And across from me was post, a German post, maybe some from the-- they want to shot. But when I ran on the field-- I have to show it to you how they-- see, I running from here, and here was a post, here a post, here [INAUDIBLE]. They're shooting against me, but they didn't know who I am. They didn't know that's a dog runs, or a fox or something. So when they start to shoot, I run on the snow in the cold. They stop shooting. They run. And I went a little bit further, and I made it to the woods. And I made it to the woods. When I made it to the woods, then I was there about-- I was-- that was about 10, 12 kilometer to the people I want to know-- I want to see, to the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they was the partisans. They was my partisans, Polacks, all the Polacks. When they saw me, they didn't know what to do with me. So right away, the next day, they brought me-- with my picture from the-- who's going to find me they thought.

speaker: interviewee
text: I surprised them. I ran away from the castle. I surprised them. So I came to my people, and they saw right away that my picture is there. And they're going to get a prize, so, so, so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: A reward?

speaker: interviewee
text: --for me. And they thought I'm something, a general from Moscow, you know. I don't know what they did. And I was with them. When I was with them, I was there about seven, eight days with them. And I did my duty, what I should do with them, with the partisans. We did this, we did that. All kind of stuff against the Germans, you know. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you be a little bit more specific, what kind of stuff you did against the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, otherwise we couldn't let go from one town to other town, the Germans. The Germans used to ride with bicycles. We used to kill them on the road. So that's all. And from that time, I was with-- but I don't believe the Polacks too. See, today they need me, they want me and I'm of use. And tomorrow, there can be a difference, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in the woods. And I was looking around with the one Polack, you know, me and him. And then we find a boy hiding in the bushes. So I took my rifle, I want to shoot. I thought maybe he was a German, I thought. And I saw a Jewish boy hiding in the bushes. I looked at him and I says-- he didn't look like Jewish. I says, what you doing here? I say, you're Jewish. I say, no, I'm not Jewish. I say, you're Jewish, because I'm Jewish, I told him. Don't be afraid of me. Don't be afraid. I say, can you show me where more Jewish people? He saw that I'm Jewish. And he saw that I'm a partisan from the Polacks. See? So they took me a little bit further, about a half kilometer from that place. They took me to a place where lots of Jewish people were. Maybe 50 or 60 children, and women and men, you know, were staying there in that hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: From that, the Russians they came already, and that's the story. That's the story. Then I went from that. From there I went, took Krzemieniec back. And I went-- they took me to Rovno. They took me to Russia. They start to work, and I was in the army in Russia. That's the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was in Russian [INAUDIBLE] after the partisans, after the woods. See, this is a little sweater-- this is a little sweater what I put in on my fate, on the way from Sobieski Castle, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were liberated by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Russians, yeah. I liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your experiences after the war that eventually brought you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me-- I went to Rovno. From Rovno I went-- they took me to Vinnytsia. Vinnytsia, I was working like a building for the soldiers homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks. Then they took me to the army. I met my wife at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I was in Korosten. I was in Korosten. And I was working for the-- you know, for the barracks, you know. And then in the night-- in the evening we used to go to the town, to Korosten.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1944, 1944. See, I was on the-- in Korosten. I saw two girls. They're walking around on the bazaar. What they going to buy? They want to buy something. I put potatoes. They didn't have money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I saw right away two girls, my wife and her sister. She lives downstairs. I say, you Jewish? I say, yeah. I thought I'm the only young Jew around here. I didn't know there were Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say-- they came from Baku, from-- they was evacuated. Far, far east. But when the Russian went back, they came back to the town. We meet each other in Korosten. So I went there, and I see this lovely girl for me. [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] I got married. And we went. I put down two rubles. She put down two rubles. We went to [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] is a place where you get married, like in Las Vegas, you know. And we got married, and that's it. And from that time, my lovely wife, we are together so many, many years. We have a lovely life. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find any of your family that survived after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No. I didn't find nobody. I didn't find nobody. I went to my town. I thought maybe I'll find something from my neighbors, the Polacks. And, see, they got the-- they occupied my-- the house what we have. And she said I'll give you 20,000 zlotys. For 20,000 zlotys I could buy a piece of bread with a pair of pants. But I had the [INAUDIBLE] from Russia. So I took a picture of me, picture of me. I have to have a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: A passport. So I went and I took the passport. I had the pictures made. And then I went to Waldenburg. From Waldenburg I went to Szczecin. From Szczecin I went to Berlin. And from Berlin I came here. I came here to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here in 1949, in September. 9Th of September. And America was good to me. I make a little success. I make a good living. I used to work reupholstering. I was in business for myself. With my wife, I make a little success. I start to build a building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a family? Do you have children, grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two children, two daughters. I have three grandchildren, wonderful people. Wonderful girls and boys. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, maybe we can take a look at some of your pictures--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of your remembrances. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Ben, would you introduce us to the woman on your left, and also show us what you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my wife. I married my wife in 1944, the end of '44. A lovely wife. Good life together. And I'm very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: This little sweater-- can I show the sweater? This sweater, when I was in the castle, in the jail and they took away my shoes-- that's my-- they took away my shoes. And this little sweater, I took apart from both sides and I put on my foot. And this helped me to run away in the snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a very important sweater. When I married my wife, I told her to sew together this sweater because that was my memory what I-- from the jail. That's the sweater.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And, Paula, do you remember meeting Ben for the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me just about it briefly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't think I remember anything. Everything is blacked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Then let's take a look at some of the pictures then. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Who is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture. On the right side is my mother. In the middle--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your mother's name? [? Chiadla ?] [? Chiadla ?] Roter. And who is she standing next to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to her is my grandmother, my mother's mother. My grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Chia ?] [? Fryda. ?] And next to the bubbe, to bubbe, is [? Madel. ?] I don't know. And next to the left is my aunt. She used to live in-- she's went to-- to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Seattle?

speaker: interviewee
text: To America, to Seattle. She used to live in Seattle. I believe she lived in 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It must be before the war when she was, oh, I would say it was maybe 1912 or 1911.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pulawy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is different picture of my family. This is my father and mother. In the right side is my oldest sister [? Zeicl ?] In the middle that's me, Ben Roter. And on the left side is [INAUDIBLE] the oldest. And the baby is [? Chia ?] [? Fryda. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother and father, what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Roter and [? Chiadla ?] Roter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, when I was five years old. Must be 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pulawy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was in a place where they make pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Photography studio.

speaker: interviewee
text: Photography, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture is me and my two younger sisters, [? Chia ?] [? Fryda ?] and [? Mindel ?] Roter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is-- who's who? On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is [? Chia ?] [? Fryda, ?] the older one. On the right is the youngest sister, [? Mindel ?] Roter. In the middle that's me, Ben Roter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was taken [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I would say about when I was 12 years old, or 13 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. And who is this gentleman?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, Ben Roter, after the war. That was a picture from Germany. I made the picture in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the survivors from Pulawy people, you know. We met each other in Munich and we made a picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a picture in, I would say in '19-- two years later, when I came. I came in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And where are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the left, top. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben, who are these people that you're with?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my daughter and my granddaughter in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freda. Freda Jackson. And this is Amy Jackson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, Freda, do you have anything to say about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we're lucky that he's here. And he had a tough life. He's made a wonderful life for us here. He's always been the center of our family. That's all that matters to him is his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, Ben, do you have any final thoughts on the experience, your family, that you'd like to leave us with? Anything in particular you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, about my kids?

speaker: interviewer
text: About your kids, about your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have lovely kids. I have two daughters, and three grand-- a grandson-- one grandson and two granddaughters. Lovely people, smart and wise. And I hope I'm going to be happy further too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Me too.
